title: LIFE CHANGING Lessons From A Monk Turned Millionaire
description: From sleeping on train platforms in India to becoming a CEO in America, Sandeep shares the 7 most counterintuitive life lessons that changed everything. Rooted in his experience as a monk, these insights blend ancient wisdom with practical mindset shifts—from the power of stillness and identity-shaping habits to letting go of comparison and recognizing the success you already have.
transcript:
What if your biggest failure is actually
setting you up for your biggest success?
Today, I've built companies worth
hundreds of millions. But when I was a
homeless kid or fasting as a monk in
training, I never imagined these lessons
would reprogram my life. And if they
worked for someone as lost as I was,
they could work for you, too. So, if
you're just getting started, feeling
stuck, or starting over, these seven
lessons might be what you need. Lesson
number one, rock bottom is a foundation,
not a failure. Every hero's journey,
including yours, begins at rock bottom.
Broken, defeated. We've all been there.
I remember sitting by the river Ganges
in a small town called Rishies in India.
Completely lost and shattered. And my
teacher who was a great monk asked me
this very interesting question. He said,
"If I gave you watercolors and asked you
to paint on top of this flowing river,
how long would your painting last?" We
all know the answer. Almost all wisdom
traditions affirm this idea. Hindus call
it Maya. Buddhists speak of
impermanence. Islam talks about the veil
of ignorance. Western religions view our
world as temporary as well. And I'm no
expert in any of that. But here's what I
know. Modern science totally agrees. By
the time you finish the sentence, 23
million of your cells will have been
replaced. We think we're the same
person, but we're not. Even a second
later, we have changed. Another way to
think about this is that everything is
short-lived, including our setbacks and
failures. When we lose a job or face a
heartbreak, fail at something important,
watch our dreams get destroyed, it
really hurts. But as the great
songwriter Leonard Conn once wrote,
there's a crack in everything. That's
how the light gets in. One way to think
about this is when we hit rock bottom,
we have found our solid ground, our
foundation. Can't get any worse than
that. That lesson was critical in my
life because it taught me to believe
that my setbacks were not end points,
they were launching pads. And that
mindset really saved me. If one thing
doesn't work, let's try another. I have
pivoted more times than I can count.
Tech support to product to marketing to
Wall Street to executive roles to
boardrooms, getting fired, getting
hired. Some of it felt like failure, but
each one was evolution. What if you
tried this every day? Spend a moment
thinking about tomorrow. Whatever pain
you're going through, by its very
nature, short-lived. Your current
situation is not your final destination.
trick your brain to believe that your
breakthrough is inevitable and it will
be. Lesson number two, the wolf you feed
is the life you lead. This one story had
such a lasting impact on me. Young boy
asks his grandfather to tell him a story
and the old man describes a fight
between two wolves. One is made of anger
and ego and envy and greed and jealousy
and the other is love, joy, peace,
compassion, confidence. They're always
fighting, says the grandfather. And the
boy asks, "But grandpa, which one will
win?" The old man smiles and says, "The
one you feed." I have lived with
negative thinking for years about
others, about myself. Maybe you have,
too. Here's what I've discovered. We
feed the negative wolf by default. Our
brain is literally wired to obsess over
mistakes and failures. It must be the
reptilian part of our brain. And of
course, not all of us can always be
positive like Ted Lasso. Some days the
negative wolf will win. That's being
human. The goal isn't to never feed it.
Is to choose the other positive wolf at
least 51% of the time. I created this
framework to catch and label my negative
thoughts and hopefully that works for
you too. What types of negative thoughts
do I have? I call it fired. Fire the
negative thoughts. F stands for fraud.
I'm fake. I'm an impostor. That's a
negative thought. I stands for identity.
I'm lazy. I'm not cut out for this. I
can't get there. R is reading minds.
Projecting. They don't like me. They're
out to get me. E is exaggeration. I
always mess up. She's never going to
love me. I'm never going to get a job.
And D is for dooming. This ruins
everything. I will never recover from
this. Here's your three-step practice
that you can try. Number one, label your
negative thoughts. Use fired or some
framework for that to name your
thoughts. Number two, separate yourself
from your thoughts. You could either say
I am not good enough or you could say my
mind is having the thought that I am not
good enough and you may think that this
is also weird but remember this is where
all of it is happening anyway and number
three redirect. Now how do we redirect?
That brings us to our next lesson.
Lesson number three you can't change
what you can't see. When I was in monk
training we would focus on this one
phrase. Some did it for 4 hours. I could
barely do it for maybe 30 minutes. I
don't want to be yet another person
highlighting the benefits of mindfulness
and meditation. But modern neuroscience
tells us that when our brain is at rest,
it's actually sorting signals from
noise. It's doing the most important
work of making sense of our lives. You
have to give your brain a chance to do
that. The best way is to look at
yourself through your inner mirror.
Hopefully, not that magical Harry Potter
mirror that shows you what you want, the
one that is honest and kind to yourself.
This self-reflection or meditation or
mindfulness helps you build a process to
identify, label, and redirect those
negative thoughts. It also has another
advantage. Your brain has different wave
patterns. Alpha, beta, theta, gamma.
Meditation helps you access the waves
that reduce stress and reset your mental
pathways. It's like clearing the cache
on your computer and deleting all the
junk files. When you do that, your
computer runs smoother afterwards. What
if you tried this? Start with 5 minutes.
Sit with your breath using apps like
Calm or Headspace or, you know, just sit
quietly. When your mind wanders, gently
bring it back to your breath. It's less
about emptying your mind or focusing on
some mantra. It's more about training
your attention. If we cannot observe our
thoughts, we don't have control over it.
And then we don't know which wolf is
getting fed. As someone who's been
completely powerless and hopeless in the
past and now sitting in boardrooms
approving million-dollar decisions, I
can tell you this much. Self-reflection
is your superpower. Lesson number four,
3 minutes. That's all it took for
someone to change my life. I had scored
34 out of 700 on my finals. Not out of
100, out of 700. I had failed in every
single course in my final year. I was
feeling completely defeated. So, one
afternoon I was sitting in the cafeteria
drowning in self-doubt and self-pity and
drinking chai with my friend Balvi. I
just shook my head and told her, "I just
don't get this stuff. All this math is
gibberish to me." She took a sip of chai
and said, "It's not that hard, dude.
You're just overthinking it. Here, let
me show you." So she opened her notebook
and walked me through one of the
mathematical proofs and step by step
something clicked inside of me for the
first time. I saw the beauty, the
elegance and the poetry and I fell in
love with math. That year I studied like
never before and got into a master's
program with full scholarship. Now I'm
not sharing this to show off how smart I
was or something like that. I wasn't.
that all it took was 3 minutes and
someone who believed in me more than I
believed in myself. So, here's what you
can do. Find someone who sees your
potential. Someone who believes in what
you're going to become, especially when
you can't see it. Now, maybe you do have
that person or maybe you don't. Either
way, it's okay. Guides and mentors can
come from anywhere. your family, your
extended family, your friends, your
professional network, your neighborhood,
your alumni network, your church or
temple community. Put yourself out there
in those learning environments. Be
curious about other people's journey.
Ask them how did they get there? Because
these little moments of grace can change
everything. Lesson number five, your tob
list matters as much as your to-do list.
We are all obsessed with to-do lists. We
all love productivity hacks. Some even
make their to have lists. Goals, dreams,
titles. I learned something from Jay
Shetty that I thought was brilliant. And
he says the most important list is your
2B list. Research shows that when we tie
our habits to who we want to become
rather than what we want, we're more
likely to stick with them. James Clear
shows this, too. I want to lose 20
pounds is less motivating than the
deeper reason I want to be healthy. And
here's why. You can only control how you
show up, not how the world shows up for
you. If you are raising money for your
startup, you can be prepared, you can be
excited, and you can be authentic. You
can't control if the investor will write
you a check. If you're dating someone
new, you can be genuine and attentive
and confident, but you can't control if
there is mutual chemistry. And same goes
for job interviews. You can be the best
smartle particle in the universe, but
you can't guarantee you will get hired.
Now, I'm not saying we should all throw
away our to-do lists, but here's what I
noticed. People who only have to-do
lists burn out. People who only have to
have lists get frustrated. But people
with 2B lists, they tend to stay more
focused and more centered. What if you
tried this? Every morning when you're
making your coffee and you're making
your to-do list, write down one word.
How do you want to show up today?
Patient or curious or present or
helpful. Experiment with this way of
being for one day and see what happens.
The irony here is that your to be list
will help you complete all your to-do
lists and your to have lists. Lesson
number six, there is always someone
better. I frequently compare myself to
people who have more better looks,
better abs, bigger paychecks, flashier
cars. I guess we all do it. And social
media has turned this into an art form.
Everyone's busy curating their lives as
a highlight reel to make everyone else
feel like they're missing out. A recent
Pew Research study found that 25% of
teenage girls say social media hurts
their mental health and 45% of teens say
it hurts their sleep habits. Think about
this for a second. If external success
guaranteed happiness, Brad Pitt and
Angelina Jolie would still be together,
right? I mean, on paper they have
everything, looks, talent, money, fame.
Yet, even that wasn't enough. What's the
point in comparing ourselves with others
on these superficial factors? If you
look at all the elite performers,
whether they're athletes or artists or
entrepreneurs, you'll see one pattern.
They care about only one thing. Getting
better today than yesterday. That's why
they win. What's your practice? Try
this. When you catch yourself in a
comparison trap, ask, "What's one thing
I am better at today than I was last
month?" could be that I spoke up in the
meeting or I tried a new recipe. Build
your personal scoreboard and you can
connect it to your 2B list from lesson
number five or make it a part of your
self-reflection practice from lesson
number three. All of this is connected.
If today is better than yesterday,
you're already winning. Lesson number
seven, are we searching for something we
already have? There is a story told in
many cultures about a musk deer. This
deer produces a beautiful scent from its
own body, but it doesn't realize its
source. So, it gets captivated by its
fragrance and frantically searches
everywhere for its source. It runs
through forests. It crashes into trees
and it exhausts itself trying to find
where this amazing scent is coming from.
And it will die searching, never
realizing that the source was within all
along. Here's one of the biggest myths
that we ought to question. We all
believe that success creates happiness.
So we frantically hunt for success,
hoping happiness will follow. But maybe
it works the opposite way. Maybe real
skills create happiness and happiness
and joy create success. I'm by no means
singing don't worry, be happy. When
you're struggling to pay rent or facing
real challenges in life, those empty
slogans don't mean much. If you want to
be successful, you'll still need to work
hard. You'll need to build your network,
develop your skills, all that is given.
But maybe if you do the same thing, but
with a genuine sense of joy, you'll be
amazed how quickly the act itself will
change. Your joy might make it slightly
easier for you to cut through pain. When
you catch yourself searching for
something external to make you happy, a
promotion, a relationship, a purchase,
then pause for a bit and ask, "Am I
running through the forest looking for
something I already carry?" There is
tremendous power in that pause. And the
answer to that question might surprise
you. Here are two things I want to
share. First, your best, most fulfilled
life doesn't require you to shave your
head and live under a tree like a monk.
I know more than enough monks and
millionaires who are unhappy and
unfulfilled. But if you build the right
mindset and rituals and habits, you can
live a life with monklike clarity and
also become a millionaire, at least
inside at the same time. And second,
these seven lessons are more like seven
invitations. You don't have to apply
them all. Just start with one that
speaks to you. I hope you found it
helpful, and if you did, please share it
with someone who needs it. I'll see you
next week.
